title: Oxylobium lanceolatum
intro: Oxylobium lanceolatum, commonly known as the golden bell, is a shrub native to the eastern coast of Australia. This plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its purported health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which are said to aid in the treatment of various conditions such as sore throats and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Oxylobium lanceolatum include sesquiterpenes and phenolic compounds, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Oxylobium lanceolatum is often prepared as a decoction or tincture, and is also used to make tea, which is said to promote relaxation and ease coughs.
taxonomy: Oxylobium lanceolatum is classified within the family Fabaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as legumes. This family is further divided into several subfamilies, with Oxylobium lanceolatum belonging to the subfamily Faboideae. Within the subfamily Faboideae, Oxylobium lanceolatum is a member of the tribe Podalyrieae. This tribe is native to the Southern Hemisphere, with Oxylobium lanceolatum specifically found in Australia. The tribe Podalyrieae is characterized by its unique flower structure and the presence of a distinctive calyx.
